deck.name: Psychology & Human Behavior
deck.description: Explore the mind and human interactions with vocabulary for feelings, personality, and behavior.

cards.definition: a shy, reticent person who prefers to spend time alone or in small groups
cards.context: As an introvert, he finds large social gatherings quite draining.

cards.definition: an outgoing, socially confident person
cards.context: She is a natural extrovert and loves being the center of attention.

cards.definition: the ability to understand and share the feelings of another
cards.context: Empathy is a crucial skill for building strong relationships.

cards.definition: a person whose personality has features typical of both an introvert and an extrovert
cards.context: Being an ambivert, he enjoys socializing but also needs his alone time.

cards.definition: the capacity to recover quickly from difficulties; toughness
cards.context: Children often show incredible resilience in the face of adversity.

cards.definition: a deeply distressing or disturbing experience
cards.context: Therapy can be helpful for processing past trauma.

cards.definition: relating to the mental action or process of acquiring knowledge and understanding
cards.context: Cognitive behavioral therapy (CBT) is a common type of talk therapy.

cards.definition: the part of the mind which is not fully aware but which influences one's actions and feelings
cards.context: Our subconscious fears often influence our daily decisions.

cards.definition: aware of and responding to one's surroundings; awake
cards.context: I made a conscious effort to be more polite to him.

cards.definition: the process of caring for and encouraging the growth or development of someone
cards.context: The 'nature vs nurture' debate explores how much our genes and our environment shape us.

cards.definition: the way in which one acts or conducts oneself, especially toward others
cards.context: Her behavior at the party was quite strange.

cards.definition: the way in which something is regarded, understood, or interpreted
cards.context: Our perception of reality can be biased by our past experiences.

cards.definition: the reason or reasons one has for acting or behaving in a particular way
cards.context: What's your motivation for learning a new language?

cards.definition: confidence in one's own worth or abilities; self-respect
cards.context: Building self-esteem is an important part of personal growth.

cards.definition: the action of delaying or postponing something
cards.context: Procrastination is the thief of time.

cards.definition: a settled or regular tendency or practice, especially one that is hard to give up
cards.context: It takes about 21 days to form a new habit.

cards.definition: something that causes a particular reaction or memory
cards.context: Certain smells can be powerful triggers for memories.

cards.definition: a feeling of worry, nervousness, or unease
cards.context: Many people experience anxiety before a big presentation.

cards.definition: a mood disorder that causes a persistent feeling of sadness and loss of interest
cards.context: Clinical depression is a serious medical condition that requires treatment.

cards.definition: a mark of disgrace associated with a particular circumstance, quality, or person
cards.context: There is still a lot of social stigma surrounding mental health issues.

cards.definition: the state of being comfortable, healthy, or happy
cards.context: Physical activity contributes to overall well-being.

cards.definition: treatment intended to relieve or heal a disorder
cards.context: Goin to therapy can provide tools for managing stress and emotions.

cards.definition: a strategy to manage or deal with difficult situations or emotions
cards.context: Exercise is a healthy coping mechanism for many people.

cards.definition: excessive interest in or admiration of oneself and one's physical appearance
cards.context: Narcissism can make it difficult to maintain healthy relationships.

cards.definition: the belief in or practice of disinterested and selfless concern for the well-being of others
cards.context: Volunteering is a great way to practice altruism.

cards.definition: compliance with standards, rules, or laws; behavior in accordance with socially accepted conventions
cards.context: Peer pressure can often lead to social conformity.

cards.definition: preconceived opinion that is not based on reason or actual experience
cards.context: We must work together to fight prejudice and discrimination.

cards.definition: a widely held but fixed and oversimplified image or idea of a particular type of person or thing
cards.context: Stereotypes about different cultures are often inaccurate and harmful.

cards.definition: a form of psychological manipulation where a person seeks to make someone doubt their own memories or perception
cards.context: Gaslighting is a serious form of emotional abuse.

cards.definition: the persistent inability to believe that one's success is deserved or has been legitimately achieved
cards.context: Many high achievers struggle with imposter syndrome.

cards.definition: a state of emotional, physical, and mental exhaustion caused by excessive and prolonged stress
cards.context: If you don't take a break, you're headed for burnout.

cards.definition: the recognition or or affirmation that a person's feelings or opinions are valid or worthwhile
cards.context: Everyone needs a bit of validation from time to time.

cards.definition: limits that a person creates to protect their own well-being and safety within relationships
cards.context: Setting healthy boundaries is key to a balanced life.

cards.definition: the way a person relates to others in close relationships
cards.context: Your attachment style is often formed in early childhood.

cards.definition: the quality or state of being exposed to the possibility of being attacked or harmed, either physically or emotionally
cards.context: Showing vulnerability is actually a sign of strength.

cards.definition: the practice of being fully present and engaged in the moment
cards.context: Mindfulness can help reduce stress and improve focus.

cards.definition: the ability to understand something immediately, without the need for conscious reasoning
cards.context: She had a strong intuition that something was wrong.

cards.definition: the psychological defense mechanism of attributing one's own unacceptable feelings or traits to someone else
cards.context: He was projecting his own insecurities onto his friend.

cards.definition: having the ability to find quick and clever ways to overcome difficulties
cards.context: Being resourceful is a great trait to have in an emergency.

cards.definition: showing or characterized by quick and inventive verbal humor
cards.context: She is known for her witty comments and sharp sense of humor.

cards.definition: not willing to listen to or tolerate other people's views; prejudiced
cards.context: Traveling can help people become less narrow-minded.

cards.definition: willing to consider new ideas; unprejudiced
cards.context: Being open-minded is essential for personal growth and learning.

cards.definition: a person who can endure pain or hardship without showing their feelings or complaining
cards.context: He remained stoic throughout the entire difficult ordeal.

cards.definition: a feeling of pensive sadness, typically with no obvious cause
cards.context: The rainy weather often makes me feel a bit melancholy.

cards.definition: extremely happy and excited
cards.context: She was elated when she heard she had got the job.
